name: Sabiñánigo - Jaca - Caldearenas Loop
name_original: Lasieso y Bernués – circular desde Sabiñánigo
description: This difficult 54.7-mile road cycling loop through the Aragonese Pyrenees offers challenging climbs and picturesque village views.
region_intro: Road cycling around Sabiñánigo offers access to the Pyrenees, characterized by diverse and dramatic landscapes. The region features deep limestone gorges, unique rock formations known as "mallos," and extensive forests. Cyclists navigate through varied terrain, including challenging mountain passes and scenic river valleys, providing a range of elevation changes. Best road cycling routes around Sabiñánigo The most popular road cycling route is Treparriscos Medio Fondo, a 51.4 miles (82.7 km) difficult trail that takes 4 hours 26 minutes to complete. This route is known for its challenging climbs and endurance demands. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sabiñánigo - Jaca - Caldearenas Loop, a difficult 54.7 miles (88.0 km) path. This route offers a scenic journey connecting several towns and features significant elevation gain. Local road cyclists also love the Sierra de Guara Norte – circular to Nocito from Sabiñánigo, a 61.9 miles (99.6 km) trail leading through the Sierra y los Cañones de Guara Natural Park, often completed in about 7 hours 36 minutes. Road cycling around Sabiñánigo is defined by its Pyrenean mountain passes, deep gorges, and unique rock formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging ascents. The routes in Sabiñánigo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 2,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Sabiñánigo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes are available around Sabiñánigo?
A: Sabiñánigo offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, catering to various skill levels. You'll find everything from moderate rides to highly challenging ascents through the Pyrenean mountain passes. The region is characterized by its dramatic landscapes, including deep limestone gorges, unique rock formations, and extensive forests, providing varied terrain and elevation changes.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Sabiñánigo is renowned for its challenging climbs and is even the starting point for the demanding Quebrantahuesos race. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e923859409/treparriscos-medio-fondo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Treparriscos Medio Fondo&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain and endurance tests. The region provides access to iconic climbs such as the Puerto del Portalet and Cotefablo Pass, which are frequently featured in professional races.

Q: Can I find easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners or less experienced riders?
A: While many routes are challenging, there are options for less experienced riders. Out of the 19 available routes, 1 is rated easy and 5 are moderate. These routes typically feature less intense climbs and offer a more relaxed way to explore the beautiful surroundings of Sabiñánigo.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The Sabiñánigo area is rich in natural beauty and historical sites. You can cycle past stunning features like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4473222" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrafita Lake&lt;/a&gt; and the panoramic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3321330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parador de Oroel Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. The region also boasts unique rock formations such as the Mallos de Riglos and deep gorges like those found in the Sierra y los Cañones de Guara Natural Park.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by waterfalls?
A: Yes, the region around Sabiñánigo features several beautiful waterfalls. While specific road cycling routes might not directly pass every waterfall, you can find highlights such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5041014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chorro Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4662298" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ip Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; in the broader area, which could be incorporated into a ride or visited as a side trip.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Sabiñánigo area?
A: Yes, several circular routes allow you to start and end in Sabiñánigo. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e930601818/sabinanigo-jaca-caldearenas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sabiñánigo - Jaca - Caldearenas Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a scenic journey connecting multiple towns. Another notable circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926854244/sierra-de-guara-norte-circular-to-nocito-from-sabinanigo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Guara Norte – circular to Nocito from Sabiñánigo&lt;/a&gt;, taking you through the stunning Sierra y los Cañones de Guara Natural Park.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Sabiñánigo?
A: The best time for road cycling in Sabiñánigo is generally from spring to autumn. During these seasons, the weather is more favorable, offering pleasant temperatures for long rides. Summer can be warm, especially in lower elevations, but the higher Pyrenean passes provide cooler conditions. Autumn brings beautiful foliage and comfortable cycling weather.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Sabiñánigo?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Sabiñánigo, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 15 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's dramatic Pyrenean landscapes, the variety of challenging mountain passes, and the opportunity to explore charming villages and historic sites like the Mozarabic and Romanesque churches of the Serrablo region.

Q: Are there routes that explore historical or cultural sites?
A: Absolutely. Routes like the 'Return to Serrablo' offer a journey through charming villages and allow cyclists to explore the unique Mozarabic and Romanesque churches of the Serrablo region. You can also find routes that pass by significant historical landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5013399" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Somport Pass&lt;/a&gt;, which marks the start of the Camino Aragonés.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Sabiñánigo area?
A: There are over 19 road cycling routes available around Sabiñánigo on komoot. These routes offer a wide range of distances and difficulty levels, ensuring there's something for every road cyclist looking to explore the Pyrenean foothills.

Q: Are there any routes that connect to nearby towns or cities?
A: Yes, routes often connect Sabiñánigo with other notable towns in the region. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e930601818/sabinanigo-jaca-caldearenas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sabiñánigo - Jaca - Caldearenas Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you through Jaca, a significant town with its own historical attractions like the Citadel. Another route, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38175146" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaca Old Town – Jaca Old Town and Citadel loop from Sabiñánigo&lt;/a&gt;, specifically highlights the historical center of Jaca.
tips: If you are interested in visiting the parish church, dedicated to San Pedro, you can try to contact the family that lives behind, who keep the key and used to show it with pleasure if they had time.
Bernués is located on a hill at an altitude of 917 meters above the old royal road from Zaragoza to Jaca, in a rugged orographic terrain of the Soduruel subregion.
Great branch line to the main road, on the whole route I saw only 2 cars. Great road conditions, a bit of undulating terrain, how made for a hearty tempo training.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Sabiñánigo - Jaca - Caldearenas Loop for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This loop is rated as "difficult" and is best suited for cyclists with good to very good fitness. You should expect significant elevation changes, including demanding climbs and exhilarating descents, characteristic of the Pyrenean terrain. The region is known for its challenging, often "Tour de France grade" climbs.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the mountainous Pyrenean terrain, the best time to cycle this loop is typically from late spring through early autumn (May to October). During these months, you'll generally find more favorable weather conditions, less chance of snow on higher passes, and milder temperatures for challenging climbs.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: Sabiñánigo is a well-known cycling hub and the starting point for the famous "La Quebrantahuesos" race, making it a practical place to begin. While specific parking lots for this exact loop aren't detailed, you should find parking options within Sabiñánigo itself.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road cycling loop through the Aragonese Pyrenees.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for cycling with dogs?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for racebikes on paved roads, often involving significant speeds and traffic. It's generally not recommended or safe to cycle with dogs on such routes. For dog-friendly outdoor activities, consider dedicated walking or mountain biking trails where dogs can be safely managed.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers stunning views of the Aragonese Pyrenees, with dramatic landscapes, forested hills, and glacial valleys. You'll pass through traditional Pyrenean villages, experiencing authentic local culture and "glorious landscapes" often off the beaten track.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several interesting spots. You'll encounter the Church of San Pedro de Lasiesto, pass through the settlement of Bernués, and ride along a section of the Sabiñánigo–Jaca Cycle Path.

faqs.question: What is the typical road surface like on this loop?
faqs.answer: Despite the mountainous terrain, many road cycling routes in this area, including this loop, feature mostly paved surfaces. This allows for a smoother ride compared to off-road trails, though road conditions can vary.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this 88 km (55 miles) loop is approximately 4 hours and 9 minutes of riding time. However, this can vary significantly based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and weather conditions.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known paths or trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other local paths. You'll find it intersects with parts of the Ruta 1 - Jaca – Bernués por Calzada Romana – Javierrelatre – Susín – Artaso – Jaca, R16 - Caldearenas, and the Camino Pastor, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the loop connects the towns of Sabiñánigo, Jaca, and Caldearenas, you will find opportunities for cafes, pubs, and accommodation in these settlements. Jaca, in particular, is a prominent town in the Pyrenees and a hub for various outdoor activities, offering more options.

waypoints.name: Church of San Pedro de Lasiesto
